ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for potato wedges; A00LC (potato products) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 226803007 represents potato product as a food concept; used as closest parent as no wedge-specific code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of potato wedges is approximately 150 g, equivalent to 6–8 large wedge-cut potato pieces, as commonly served as a side dish or snack in cafes and casual dining restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Potato, baked, flesh and skin, without salt (USDA FoodData Central ID 170032)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values are based on USDA FoodData Central entry for baked potato (flesh and skin, without salt, ID 170032) as the closest approved-source match for plain potato wedges. Sodium adjusted upward to 250 mg per 100 g to reflect typical seasoning applied in cafe-style potato wedges. Energy and macros retained from USDA baked potato baseline. This is an adaptation; exact restaurant formulation is unknown.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 75 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for baked/oven-cooked potato (white potato, baked without fat, GI ~75–85, mean ~75). Potato wedges are wedge-cut and oven-baked or shallow-fried potatoes; the GI is expected to be in the high range consistent with baked potato. GL per serving calculated as (75 × 27.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 30.4 for a 150 g serving. Classified as high GI (≥70).

hypertension.guidance: Cafe-style potato wedges typically contain 250–400 mg sodium per 100 g due to added salt and seasoning; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request unseasoned or lightly salted preparations to reduce sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index and significant carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Potato wedges have a high GI (~75) and deliver approximately 40.5 g of carbohydrate per 150 g serving, resulting in a high glycemic load of ~30; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response, and prefer baked over deep-fried preparations.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: When fried, potato wedges contribute 7 g fat per 100 g including approximately 1 g saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose oven-baked preparations with minimal oil and avoid high-sodium seasoning blends to support heart health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Potato wedges are thick, wedge-shaped cuts of potato — typically skin-on — that are seasoned with salt and spices and then either oven-baked or shallow-fried until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining soft and fluffy on the inside. They are a popular Western-style snack and side dish widely served in cafes, casual dining restaurants, and fast-food outlets across Singapore and internationally. The dish is made from whole potatoes cut into 6–8 wedge segments, tossed in oil and seasoning (commonly paprika, garlic powder, and salt), and cooked at high heat. Nutritionally, a 150 g serving provides approximately 278 kcal, 40.5 g carbohydrate, 10.5 g fat, and 3.8 g protein. Potato wedges have a high glycemic index (~75) and a high glycemic load (~30 per serving), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The sodium content from seasoning (approximately 375 mg per 150 g serving) is a consideration for those with hypertension. Potato wedges are naturally gluten-free, vegetarian, and vegan when prepared without dairy-based dips. They are a good source of potassium (~630 mg per 150 g serving) and provide modest amounts of vitamin C, vitamin B6, and dietary fibre from the skin.
common_user_queries: How many calories in potato wedges?
What is the GI of potato wedges?
How much sodium in seasoned potato wedges?
Difference between potato wedges and french fries

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA FoodData Central baked potato entry (ID 170032), which is an approved primary source for generic Western staple foods. Sodium has been adjusted upward to reflect typical cafe-style seasoning. The GI value is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for baked white potato. No exact Singapore-specific nutrient entry for potato wedges was found in HPB Singapore Foodo. Confidence is medium due to the adaptation and seasoning adjustment.
notes: Food group corrected from 'Desserts' (input) to 'Snacks' as potato wedges are a savoury snack/side dish. Cuisine corrected from 'Coffee' (input tag) to 'Western' as the primary cuisine classification. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central baked potato (ID 170032) with sodium adjusted to 250 mg/100g to reflect typical seasoning. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for seasoned potato wedges in the USDA source for this preparation. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for baked potato. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not consistently reported in the source for this preparation. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for this entry. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as potato wedges are plant-based and contain no cholesterol; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food containing no vitamin B12; naturally zero. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as no partially hydrogenated oils are used in standard potato wedge preparation; confirmed zero per USDA source. GI of 75 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous baked potato value. GL per 150 g serving = (75 × 27.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 30.4. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 used for potato wedge; FoodEx2 A00LC used as closest parent for potato products.
